narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing this path despite the challenges many face, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admission system uses a predictive model trained on data from thousands of students, looking at factors like grades, test scores, family background, and study plans to forecast the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try. For you, the model estimated a 55.9% probability of failing the bar exam, which tipped the scales just over the threshold for denial, as the school prioritizes applicants predicted to pass with high confidence. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your plan to attend part-time, which is uncommon—only about 7% of students do so—and the model sees it as a strong risk for bar success compared to the typical full-time path taken by over 92% of others. On a more positive note, your LSAT score of 33.5 and undergraduate GPA of 3.3 both worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward passing since they're competitive, especially your GPA which beats the average of 3.21. Your middle family income bracket had a moderate push toward failure risk relative to higher-income peers, while your Year 3 school decile of 4 and Year 1 decile of 5 were slightly concerning compared to typical stronger performers around the 5th or 6th deciles. Overall, your part-time status stood out as the most influential hurdle, outweighing the strengths in your test scores and GPA when stacked against students who match the dataset's averages. This doesn't reflect your full potential or the barriers you've overcome as a Black woman in academia—it's just one model's snapshot based on patterns in past data. To move forward, consider reapplying with a full-time plan if feasible, as that could dramatically shift the prediction in your favor, or boost your LSAT closer to the 36.7 average through targeted prep. You might also explore schools with more flexible models or appeal by highlighting unique strengths like your resilience and background. Keep pushing; many have turned similar predictions around with persistence. We're rooting for you.
